title: Writing - Part 2: Exercise 1
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 220-260 words.

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt: The director of a language school has asked you to write a report on how the school could attract more adult learners. Your report should identify the main barriers that prevent adults from studying, evaluate the school's current offer, and recommend new initiatives to increase enrolment among this age group.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Report on Attracting Adult Learners to Meridian Language School

Introduction
This report examines the factors that currently prevent adults from enrolling in language courses, assesses Meridian Language School's existing provision for this demographic, and proposes a series of targeted initiatives to increase adult participation.

Barriers to Adult Learning
Research and feedback from prospective students indicate three primary obstacles:
- Time constraints arising from professional and family commitments, which make fixed-schedule classes impractical.
- Financial considerations, particularly for individuals who must fund their own development without employer support.
- A lack of confidence stemming from previous negative educational experiences, especially among learners who left formal education many years ago.

Current Provision
Meridian currently offers morning and evening group classes and a limited number of individual tutoring sessions. While the teaching quality is consistently praised, the rigid timetable and lack of online options significantly restrict accessibility for working adults.

Recommendations
To attract a larger adult cohort, the following initiatives are proposed:
- Launching a flexible online learning platform that allows students to complete modules at their own pace.
- Introducing an 'employer partnership' scheme to offer subsidised rates to companies investing in staff language training.
- Developing a specific 'Return to Learning' programme with smaller class sizes and a supportive, confidence-building curriculum for adults who have been out of education for an extended period.

Conclusion
By addressing the structural and psychological barriers faced by adult learners, Meridian Language School has significant potential to grow its enrolment within this underserved and professionally valuable demographic.

tasks.title: Letter
tasks.prompt: Your city council is planning to demolish a historic building in the city centre to make way for a new shopping development. You and many other residents are strongly opposed to this decision. Write a letter to the council leader expressing your opposition, explaining why the building is important, and proposing an alternative solution.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Councillor Whitmore,

I am writing on behalf of the Eastgate Heritage Society to formally register our opposition to the proposed demolition of the Eastgate Corn Exchange, which I understand has been scheduled to proceed as part of the Central Quarter regeneration scheme. We believe this decision is both culturally misguided and economically shortsighted, and we urge the council to reconsider.

The Corn Exchange is one of the few surviving examples of Victorian commercial architecture in the city. Completed in 1871, it was designated a building of local architectural significance in the council's own heritage assessment of 2018. Its ornate cast-iron interior, which is rarely found in such an intact state, represents an irreplaceable record of the city's industrial and social history. Once demolished, this heritage cannot be recovered.

Furthermore, we would challenge the economic premise that demolition is the only viable route to regeneration. Several comparable buildings in other British cities—including the Leeds Corn Exchange and Manchester's Barton Arcade—have been successfully converted into thriving retail and leisure spaces. Such adaptive reuse attracts heritage tourism, supports independent businesses, and commands greater long-term commercial value than a generic shopping development.

We therefore propose that the council commission an independent feasibility study into the conversion of the Corn Exchange before any demolition is authorised. We are confident it will demonstrate that preservation and regeneration are not mutually exclusive objectives.

We look forward to your response.

Yours sincerely,
Dr. Caroline Blake
Chair, Eastgate Heritage Society

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt: An international culture magazine is inviting readers to submit reviews of a film, book, or album that they feel has been underrated or overlooked. Describe the work, explain why it deserves more recognition, and say who you think would enjoy it. Write your review.
tasks.modelAnswer:
An Overlooked Masterpiece: 'The Remains of the Day' by Kazuo Ishiguro

In a literary landscape that rewards immediacy and spectacle, Kazuo Ishiguro's 'The Remains of the Day' stands as a quiet, devastating counterpoint—a novel about restraint, regret, and the irreversible passage of time that, despite winning the Booker Prize in 1989, remains far less read than it deserves.

The novel follows Stevens, an elderly English butler, as he undertakes a motor journey across the south of England to visit a former colleague. Through his narration—measured, formal, and deeply unreliable—Ishiguro gradually reveals a life shaped by absolute professional dedication at the cost of genuine human connection. Stevens has sublimated every personal desire to an ideal of 'dignified service', and as he reflects on past choices, the reader becomes increasingly aware of what he has sacrificed without ever fully acknowledging to himself.

What is extraordinary about the novel is not what is said but what is withheld. Ishiguro's prose operates through omission and circumlocution; the emotional reality of the story exists in the spaces between sentences. It is a technique that demands patience from the reader but rewards it with an emotional impact of rare and lingering intensity.

This is not a novel for those seeking dramatic plot or fast-paced narrative. It is, however, essential reading for anyone interested in the subtleties of human psychology, the ethics of service and loyalty, and the melancholy beauty of a life honestly—if belatedly—examined.
